nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward coral reefs, generally between 1 and 38 m depth, where individuals associate closely with host anemones.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-Pacific reef; sheltered lagoons and outer reef slopes with hard substrate hosting Heteractis and Stichodactyla anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
host anemone optional (Heteractis magnifica, H. crispa, Macrodactyla doreensis, Stichodactyla gigantea)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, marine-pellet/flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended.

avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
predatory fish that may eat small clownfish
triggers and large angelfish that may bully them

imageCandidates.notes: portioid; 2048x1620. Sharp full lateral view showing the complete diagnostic white dorsal stripe running head to tail plus the white vertical head bar on a vibrant pink/salmon body, in host anemone.
Jenny (JennyHuang) from Taipei; 1520x2064. Tack-sharp, highly vibrant close portrait with crisp white head bar and dorsal stripe; caudal fin partly obscured by anemone.
Jay Paroline; 2048x1536. Whole fish in-situ over reef anemone, different angle for variety; pink body with clear white head bar and dorsal stripe.

summary: A peaceful pink-and-white anemonefish from the Indo-West Pacific, generally considered one of the calmer clownfish; reef-safe and commonly hosted by Heteractis magnifica and similar anemones.
careNotes: In the wild the Skunk Clownfish associates with Heteractis magnifica, H. crispa, Macrodactyla doreensis, and Stichodactyla gigantea, but it does not require a host anemone in captivity and will readily shelter among live rockwork instead. Like all clownfish, it is a protandrous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning fish start life capable of functioning as males and only the dominant individual in a pair transitions to female. In a bonded pair the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male; this sex change has been reported around 5.4 cm total length. The Skunk Clownfish is best kept as a single specimen or as a mated pair. Housing multiple individuals of this species, or mixing it with other clownfish species, generally leads to serious aggression. Minimum tank size recommendations vary across sources — figures from 113 L upward are commonly cited for a single fish, with larger volumes advised for a bonded pair — but a tank of around 120 L or more is broadly considered adequate to comfortably house a pair alongside a host anemone and reef rockwork.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity but is generally reported as more challenging than Ocellaris and Percula Clownfish. The Skunk Clownfish is an egg-layer that deposits eggs on a cleaned flat surface near the host anemone; eggs typically hatch in 8 to 11 days depending on temperature. Larvae require very small live foods — rotifers first, then enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp — and a dedicated rearing tank separate from the display aquarium.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Bleeker, 1855), family (Pomacentridae), max size (10 cm TL), preferred temperature range (25.2-29°C), Indo-West Pacific distribution, protandrous hermaphroditism, and host anemone associations.

additional.name: Reeflings species profile
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of tank-size range (20-30 US gal minimum, 40 US gal for a pair), peaceful temperament, reef-safe behaviour, and host anemone list. Used to corroborate the cross-source average for temperature (75-82°F band).

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary; the per-species wiki URL returned HTTP 403, so used as a slice-level reference only. Fishkeeping World (404) was also unavailable; community Reeflings profile substituted for cross-verification.

fish.breedingNotes: Reported as harder to spawn and rear than Ocellaris and Percula Clownfish; larvae require rotifer cultures and enriched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
